summary: My mother migrated from Somalia when the Somali Civil War happened, so I never lived there myself. I only know about Somalia through the stories my parents shared with me. Many people, including my mother, fled Somalia due to the ongoing conflict and ended up in refugee camps in Kenya. Even now, Somalia is not a safe place to return to, which is why many families remain displaced.

I was actually born in Kenya and lived there until I was twelve years old. My family and I stayed in a refugee camp there with many other Somali people after fleeing the civil war. I lived with my grandma, aunt, and uncle in the camp, which was a tough but loving environment. Kenya was where my mother started the process of applying for us to resettle in the United States around 2006.

My whole family was resettled in the United States when I was twelve years old, after my mother’s application was approved. We arrived at JFK Airport in New York City, tired but excited to start a new life. I remember the kindness of a woman who gave my dad some money when we first arrived. Eventually, we settled in Buffalo, New York, where we began adjusting to American life and school.

The Somali Civil War was the reason my mother left Somalia and came to Kenya as a refugee. It caused so much instability and danger that many families, including mine, had no choice but to flee. The war has lasted for many years, and even now, it’s not safe for people to return, which keeps many refugees in camps for decades.

Before coming to the United States, my family had to leave the refugee camp and travel to Nairobi, which was about an eight-hour drive. We stayed in a building there for about ten days while waiting for our departure. Nairobi was a place where we saw many different kinds of people for the first time, which was both exciting and overwhelming.

We arrived at JFK Airport in New York City after leaving Nairobi and the refugee camp. I remember how tired we were and how we ended up sleeping on the floor at the airport. A kind woman approached my dad, gave him some money, and welcomed us, which made a big impression on us during that first day in America.

After arriving in the United States, my family settled in Buffalo, New York. It was there that we started adjusting to our new life, including going to school, which was very different from my experience in Kenya. Our caseworker helped us get settled, although she was on vacation when we arrived, so someone else had to assist us initially.
